summary: Irish playwright, poet, and author known for The Importance of Being Earnest, The Picture of Dorian Gray, and 'The Ballad of Reading Gaol.' Imprisoned for homosexuality.

works.name: The Importance of Being Earnest
works.description: Wilde's most famous play. Lady Bracknell exclaims 'a handbag?!' when questioning Jack Worthing about being found as a baby at Victoria station. Jack proves he was abandoned there and has the necessary name to marry Gwendolen Fairfax. Miss Prism swapped the baby. Robert Hichens's novel The Green Carnation is titled for the flower Wilde supposedly told followers to wear for the premiere. Tom Stoppard's Travesties involves a lawsuit between Joyce and Henry Carr over a staging of this play.

clues.clue: Lady Bracknell exclaims 'a handbag?!' about Jack's upbringing
examples: One of this author's characters exclaims 'a handbag?!'

clues.clue: Jack proves he was abandoned at Victoria station; needs correct name to marry Gwendolen Fairfax
examples: Jack proves that he was abandoned as a baby at Victoria station

clues.clue: Green carnation associated with premiere; Hichens novel
examples: A novel by Robert Hichens is titled for the flower that this author supposedly told his followers to wear

works.name: The Picture of Dorian Gray
works.description: Wilde's only novel, in which the artist Basil Hallward creates a portrait of the beautiful Dorian Gray that ages and decays instead of Dorian himself. The preface declares 'All art is quite useless' and compares dislike of Realism and Romanticism to 'different rages of Caliban.' Dorian reads a 'poisonous French novel' (likely Huysmans's A rebours) given by Lord Henry Wotton. Dorian poses for Hallward, murders him, and eventually stabs the portrait, dying himself. A 'yellow book' is read by Dorian. Albert Lewin's film adaptation uses Technicolor for Ivan Albright's grotesque painting. A long-lost uncensored version included more explicit romance between Dorian and Basil. Andre Gide's autobiography discusses Wilde and Dorian Gray.

clues.clue: Preface declares 'All art is quite useless'; compares Realism/Romanticism to 'rages of Caliban'
examples: declared that 'all art is quite useless' in the preface
compared the dislike of Realism and Romanticism to different 'rages of Caliban'

clues.clue: Basil Hallward creates portrait that ages instead of Dorian; Dorian murders Basil and stabs the portrait

the title character leaves the actress Sibyl and murders the artist Basil Hallward

examples: the 'yellow book' is read by a man who poses for Basil Hallward

clues.clue: Albert Lewin film uses Technicolor for Ivan Albright's painting
examples: An earlier black-and-white film adaptation used Technicolor to emphasize Ivan Albright's version of the title object

clues.clue: Long-lost uncensored version had explicit 'romance of feelings' between Dorian and Basil
examples: A long-lost 'uncensored' version included passages on a more explicit 'romance of feelings' between the title character and Basil Hallward

works.name: Lady Windermere's Fan
works.description: A play in which Mrs. Erlynne claims to have accidentally taken Lady Windermere's fan found in Lord Darlington's rooms to create a distraction, allowing Lady Windermere to slip away unnoticed. Lord Darlington says 'we are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.' Lady Windermere says she has 'something of the Puritan in me.' Guy Domville by Henry James premiered on the same opening night and was overshadowed.

clues.clue: Mrs. Erlynne claims to have taken the fan by mistake so Lady Windermere can escape Lord Darlington's rooms
examples: Mrs. Erlynne claims to have accidentally taken it in order to create a distraction

clues.clue: 'we are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars' — Lord Darlington
examples: 'we are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars' is said by Lord Darlington

clues.clue: Lady Windermere locks a diamond brooch on another character's wrist to retrieve an incriminating letter
examples: One of this author's characters locks a stolen diamond brooch on another character's wrist to get back an incriminating letter

works.name: An Ideal Husband
works.description: A play in which the government minister Sir Robert Chiltern, who made his fortune from insider trading, is blackmailed by Mrs. Cheveley into supporting a fraudulent canal scheme in Argentina. On its opening night, it overshadowed Henry James's Guy Domville.

clues.clue: Government minister blackmailed about insider trading into supporting fraudulent Argentine canal scheme
examples: a government minister who made his fortune from insider trading is blackmailed into supporting a fraudulent canal scheme in Argentina

clues.clue: Opening night overshadowed Henry James's Guy Domville
examples: On this play's opening night, its author watched a performance of An Ideal Husband, which overshadowed this unpopular play

works.name: A Woman of No Importance
works.description: A play ending with the Puritan Hester Worsley becoming engaged to Gerald Arbuthnot, the illegitimate son of Lord Illingworth. Herbert Beerbohm Tree appeared in the production. An Archdeacon whose wife has multiple disabilities appears in the play. Lord Illingworth claims not to believe in Puritan women. Alan Bennett adapted it for Talking Heads.

clues.clue: Hester Worsley becomes engaged to Lord Illingworth's illegitimate son Gerald Arbuthnot
examples: the Puritan Hester Worsley becomes engaged to the illegitimate son of Lord Illingworth

clues.clue: 'a well-tied tie is the first serious step in life' — from this play
examples: The line 'a well-tied tie is the first serious step in life' appears in this author's play A Woman of No Importance

works.name: Salome
works.description: Written in French for Sarah Bernhardt. Aubrey Beardsley drew illustrations for the English version after Wilde liked his drawing of a woman kissing a severed head. Beardsley's woodblock-like drawing features a woman in feather headdress glaring at an androgynous 'Syrian.'

clues.clue: Written in French for Sarah Bernhardt; Aubrey Beardsley illustrated the English version
examples: Aubrey Beardsley drawings accompanied the English version of a play this author wrote in French for Sarah Bernhardt

clues.clue: Wilde liked Beardsley's drawing of a woman kissing a severed head, asked him to illustrate it
examples: After liking a drawing of a woman kissing a severed head, this author asked Aubrey Beardsley to illustrate the play

works.name: "The Ballad of Reading Gaol" and Prison Writings
works.description: Wilde was imprisoned at Reading Gaol for homosexuality. 'The Ballad of Reading Gaol' declares 'each man kills the thing he loves' — 'the coward does it with a kiss / The brave man with a sword!' The prisoners look 'with a wistful eye' at the 'little tent of blue' called the sky. De Profundis was written in prison as a letter to his lover 'Bosie' (Lord Alfred Douglas), asserting 'Christ's place indeed is with the poets.' Patti Smith read from De Profundis at Reading in 2016. After release, Wilde adopted the pseudonym Sebastian Melmoth (after Saint Sebastian and Melmoth the Wanderer). His tomb at Pere Lachaise Cemetery, designed by Jacob Epstein with a sphinxlike creature, had to have a glass barrier to prevent lipstick damage.

clues.clue: 'each man kills the thing he loves' — 'the coward does it with a kiss / The brave man with a sword!'
examples: declared 'the coward does it with a kiss / The brave man with a sword!' in a poem concluding 'each man kills the thing he loves'

clues.clue: De Profundis written in prison, addressed to 'Bosie'; asserts 'Christ's place indeed is with the poets'

clues.clue: Tomb at Pere Lachaise designed by Jacob Epstein; glass barrier installed to prevent lipstick kisses
examples: A glass barrier was installed in front of a sculpture of this man in the Pere Lachaise Cemetery

clues.clue: Adopted pseudonym Sebastian Melmoth after prison
examples: After leaving Reading Gaol, Oscar Wilde took a pseudonym referencing both this person and Melmoth the Wanderer

clues.clue: Seamus Heaney argued the form reflected how Wilde 'mutated into a propagandist' in the tradition of his mother
examples: a critic argued that the form of a poem titled for this place reflected how its author 'mutated into a propagandist'

works.name: Short Stories
works.description: Wilde's short stories include 'The Happy Prince' (a swallow distributes a statue's jewels to the poor instead of going to Egypt), 'The Canterville Ghost' (an American family uses Pinkerton's Champion Stain Remover on ghostly blood stains), 'Lord Arthur Savile's Crime' (a man tries to kill his uncle with an exploding Liberty-figurine clock to fulfill a palm-reading prophecy by Septimus Podgers), and 'The Remarkable Rocket' (a firework claims 'he knew he should make a great sensation' but explodes unnoticed, parodying James Whistler).

clues.clue: 'The Happy Prince': swallow distributes statue's jewels to the poor instead of going to Egypt
examples: wrote a children's story in which a swallow planning to go to Egypt distributes a statue's jewels to the poor

clues.clue: 'The Canterville Ghost': American family uses Pinkerton's Champion Stain Remover on ghostly blood
examples: an American family uses Pinkerton's Champion Stain Remover to remove ghostly blood stains

clues.clue: 'Lord Arthur Savile's Crime': exploding Liberty clock fulfills palm reader Septimus Podgers's prophecy
examples: a man tries to kill his uncle with an exploding Liberty-figurine clock to fulfill a prophecy by the palm reader Septimus Podgers

clues.clue: 'The Remarkable Rocket': firework says he'll 'create a great sensation' but explodes unnoticed
examples: a firework claims he 'knew [he] should make a great sensation' after exploding unnoticed

works.name: Essays and Criticism
works.description: Wilde's essays include 'The Decay of Lying' (Vivian criticizes 'our monstrous worship of facts'; Cyril prompts him to argue 'Life imitates art far more than Art imitates life'), 'Pen, Pencil, and Poison' (discusses Thomas Griffiths Wainewright and claims 'a mask tells us more than a face'), and 'The Soul of Man under Socialism' (endorses socialism for strengthening artistic individualism; remarks 'To live is the rarest thing in the world'). Wilde was associated with the Decadent movement and The Yellow Book. He became a US celebrity when Richard 'Oily' Carte sent him on an 1882 lecture tour so Americans could compare Reginald Bunthorne from Gilbert and Sullivan's Patience to a real-life aesthete. Napoleon Sarony photographed him on a Persian carpet.

clues.clue: 'The Decay of Lying': 'Life imitates art far more than Art imitates life'
examples: Vivian criticizes 'our monstrous worship of facts' before Cyril prompts him to argue that 'Life imitates art'

clues.clue: 'Pen, Pencil, and Poison': discusses Wainewright; 'a mask tells us more than a face'
examples: argues that 'a mask tells us more than a face' in an essay that analyzes Thomas Griffiths Wainewright

clues.clue: 'The Soul of Man under Socialism': 'To live is the rarest thing in the world'
examples: remarks that 'To live is the rarest thing in the world'

clues.clue: 1882 US lecture tour for Gilbert and Sullivan's Patience; photographed by Napoleon Sarony
examples: Americans needed a real-life person to compare the character Reginald Bunthorne to
photographed on a Persian carpet holding a book by celebrity photographer Napoleon Sarony

comprehensive_summary:
Oscar Wilde is an Irish playwright, novelist, poet, and essayist who is among the most frequently clued literary figures in quizbowl. His most recognizable giveaway is The Importance of Being Earnest, featuring Lady Bracknell's exclamation 'a handbag?!' and Jack Worthing's discovery that he was abandoned at Victoria station. The Picture of Dorian Gray is his only novel, whose preface declares 'All art is quite useless' and compares reactions to Realism and Romanticism to 'different rages of Caliban.' The portrait painted by Basil Hallward ages instead of Dorian, who reads a 'yellow book' from Lord Henry Wotton. Albert Lewin's film adaptation uses Technicolor for Ivan Albright's grotesque painting.

Wilde's other major plays include Lady Windermere's Fan (Mrs. Erlynne's sacrifice, 'we are all in the gutter'), An Ideal Husband (blackmail over insider trading), A Woman of No Importance (Hester Worsley and Lord Illingworth), and Salome (written in French for Sarah Bernhardt, illustrated by Aubrey Beardsley). His short stories include 'The Happy Prince' (swallow distributing jewels), 'The Canterville Ghost' (Pinkerton's Stain Remover), and 'Lord Arthur Savile's Crime' (exploding clock).

Wilde was imprisoned at Reading Gaol for homosexuality. 'The Ballad of Reading Gaol' declares 'each man kills the thing he loves.' De Profundis, his prison letter to lover 'Bosie,' asserts 'Christ's place indeed is with the poets.' His tomb at Pere Lachaise, designed by Jacob Epstein, required a glass barrier due to lipstick damage. His essays include 'The Decay of Lying' ('Life imitates art') and 'The Soul of Man under Socialism.' He was associated with the Decadent movement and became famous in America through an 1882 lecture tour tied to Gilbert and Sullivan's Patience.
